title: Fretting-Hand Position
description: Correct fretting-hand position — thumb behind the neck opposite the middle finger, fingers curved, pressing just behind the fret — prevents injury and enables every technique that follows. This is non-negotiable to establish first.
practice: Hold the guitar and check your thumb position in a mirror before every practice session.
Play a chromatic exercise (1-2-3-4 across all strings) focusing entirely on hand position, not speed.
Alternate between correct and incorrect thumb positions so you can feel the difference.

title: Picking Mechanics
description: Relaxed pick grip, consistent pick angle, and controlled wrist motion form the mechanical basis of all picking. Tension in the picking hand is the single most common obstacle for beginners.
practice: Hold the pick loosely enough that you could drop it if you tried to.
Pick a single string at 60 BPM for 4 minutes focusing only on relaxation.
Record your picking hand and watch for unnecessary arm or elbow movement.

title: Alternate Picking
description: Alternate picking — strict down-up-down-up — is the most efficient way to pick notes quickly and evenly. The discipline is maintaining the pattern even when crossing strings or changing rhythmic groupings.
practice: Play a chromatic pattern on one string with strict alternate picking at 60 BPM. Aim for 120 BPM over time.
Play a scale pattern on two strings with alternate picking — verify the pattern doesn't break on string changes.
Set a metronome to 80 BPM and play one note per click: down, down, up, up — then down, up, down, up. Feel the difference.

title: Hammer-Ons
description: A hammer-on sounds a note by striking the fret with a fretting finger without picking. The attack must be quick and firm — fingertip landing directly behind the fret — or the note buzzes or dies.
practice: Hammer from index finger to ring finger on the same string, maintaining even volume between the picked and hammered notes.
Practice hammer-ons at a slow tempo with the metronome until both notes sound equally loud.
Do a two-note hammer-on exercise: open string → hammer 2nd fret. All six strings.

title: Pull-Offs
description: A pull-off is the hammer-on in reverse — you pluck the string sideways with the departing finger to sound the lower note. The common error is simply lifting the finger, which produces no sound.
practice: Practice the 3rd-fret → open string pull-off until the open note rings clearly.
Combine a hammer-on and pull-off into a single slur on one picked note.
Play a trill (rapid hammer/pull alternation) for 30 seconds on each string without the pitch dying.

title: Legato Phrases
description: Legato playing strings multiple hammer-ons and pull-offs together so that only the first note of a group is picked. The result is a fluid, vocal quality that contrasts with picked notes. The challenge is maintaining volume and clarity through long legato runs.
practice: Play a four-note legato run (one pick, three hammers) on a single string. Goal: all four notes at equal volume.
Learn a two-bar pentatonic scale run using only legato.
Alternate picked phrases and legato phrases in the same solo — make both dynamics match.

title: Palm Muting
description: Palm muting lays the heel of the picking hand lightly against the strings near the bridge, dampening their sustain. It's essential for tight rhythm playing, riffs, and controlling noise.
practice: Play a single open E string with palm mute — adjust pressure until the note is percussive but still pitched.
Play an E5 power chord with and without palm mute alternating every 2 beats.
Practice a punk-style down-stroke palm-muted pattern for 2 minutes straight.

title: String Bending
description: A bend pushes or pulls the string to raise its pitch by a half or whole step. Intonation — bending to the exact target pitch — is the skill. Support the bending finger with the fingers behind it; never bend with one finger alone.
practice: Bend the B string at the 7th fret up one whole step to the D. Verify by playing the fretted D note first.
Bend up, hold for 2 beats, release. Keep the bent pitch stable — no wobble.
Play a phrase ending in a bend and vibrato. Record and listen for pitch accuracy.

title: Vibrato
description: Vibrato is a controlled oscillation of pitch — a slight, rhythmic bend and release that adds expression to sustained notes. Consistent width and speed define a player's signature vibrato. It takes months to develop but begins immediately.
practice: Sustain a note and apply vibrato for 8 full beats. Keep the oscillation even.
Try slow, wide vibrato vs fast, narrow vibrato on the same note. Develop both.
Sing the pitch you want and match it with your vibrato — use your ear as the guide.

title: Slides
description: A slide moves a fretting finger along the string while maintaining contact, so you hear a continuous glide between two notes. Keep consistent pressure — too light and the pitch dies; too heavy and it chokes.
practice: Slide from the 5th to the 7th fret on the G string, then back. Both notes should ring clearly.
Practice slides of different lengths: 1 fret, 3 frets, 5 frets.
Use a slide to connect two phrases in a blues-style lick.
